excerpt: House of Commons, p. 9, reports a Kovalev-linked list of 25,000 civilian deaths in Grozny.
transform_note: Used as the best explicit civilian direct-death anchor; reported on 7 February 1995.
confidence_note: Strongest explicit civilian wording among accessible sources, but still a compiled list rather than a complete named-victim registry.

excerpt: Hansen & Seely, p. 44, gives a lower civilian-death estimate of 15,000 for the city attack.
transform_note: Used as the low civilian direct-death anchor.
confidence_note: Conservative explicit civilian figure, but based on humanitarian synthesis rather than a complete victim register.

excerpt: HRW summarizes a Kovalev-ordered study that estimated 25,000 deaths in Grozny with a 25% error band.
transform_note: Kept outside the core direct-death path because status and direct/indirect split are not explicit; upper bound informs the high civilian-death scenario.
confidence_note: Important contextual source, but ambiguous enough that it should not replace explicit civilian direct-death claims.

excerpt: Hansen & Seely, p. 27, gives the 1989 Grozny population as 397,000.
transform_note: Used as the strongest baseline city-population anchor.
confidence_note: Good census anchor, but predates the large pre-war outflow of ethnic Slavs.

excerpt: Hansen & Seely, p. 36, says more than 100,000 ethnic Slavs, mostly from Grozny, left before the war.
transform_note: Used as a lower-bound prewar outflow prior for Grozny's start-of-battle civilian stock.
confidence_note: Lower-bound statement, not a precise city-only count.

excerpt: Hansen & Seely, p. 45, uses a pre-war Grozny population of 400,000.
transform_note: Used as the upper civilian-start scenario anchor.
confidence_note: Pre-war approximate stock; should be interpreted alongside the documented pre-December outflow.

excerpt: Derived from Hansen & Seely, p. 45: one-quarter of 400,000 implies roughly 100,000 civilians still in Grozny by mid-January.
transform_note: Arithmetic derivation from the source's own wording: 0.25 x 400,000 = 100,000 civilians remaining by mid-January.
confidence_note: Strong mid-January anchor, but still approximate because 'mid-January' is not an exact day.

excerpt: House of Commons, p. 9, relays Fred Cuny's estimate that roughly 30,000 people remained in Grozny by early March.
transform_note: Used as the end-of-episode civilian-stock anchor.
confidence_note: Important late-battle anchor, but based on an observer estimate rather than an administrative count.

excerpt: Oliker, p. 38, describes a 6,000-man Russian force that entered Grozny on New Year's Eve.
transform_note: Lower-bound Russian directly engaged assault force inside the city.
confidence_note: Very useful lower-bound combatant exposure anchor, but only for the entering assault force, not the whole city-assignment.

excerpt: Oliker, p. 38, gives a very wide early estimate for Chechen defenders: about 1,000 to about 10,000.
transform_note: Used only as a range prior for early Chechen defender presence; not used as a direct fatality measure.
confidence_note: Extremely wide and clearly uncertain, but still useful as a bounding prior.

excerpt: Thomas, p. 35, gives 23,800 men gathered when the operation started.
transform_note: Upper-bound Russian/MVD force pool directly relevant to the initial Grozny operation, though not all were necessarily inside the city at once.
confidence_note: Useful force-pool anchor; needs down-selection for direct city-assignment.

excerpt: Thomas's force-structure table gives 38,000 men involved in the operation by late December.
transform_note: Used only as an outer ceiling for force-assignment scenarios; not all 38,000 are counted in the city denominator.
confidence_note: Broad operation total; unsuitable as a direct city denominator without adjustment.

excerpt: Thomas, p. 21, reports about 3,500 Chechen militants still in Grozny on 21 January.
transform_note: Key mid-battle defender-presence anchor for the south-of-Sunzha phase.
confidence_note: Important phase anchor, but still an estimate reported in an analytical narrative.

excerpt: House of Commons, p. 8, reports an official Russian battle figure of 1,376 killed and 408 missing.
transform_note: Used as the hard lower bound for combatant direct deaths; no robust citywide Chechen fatality count was found.
confidence_note: Battle-linked official Russian fatality figure, but likely an undercount of total combatant deaths because it excludes unmeasured Chechen fighter fatalities.

excerpt: Gott, pp. 95-96, repeats the official Russian battle figure of 1,376 killed and 408 missing.
transform_note: Independent corroboration of the same official Russian battle fatality figure.

excerpt: House of Commons, p. 8, also records a lower-bound Russian wounded count above 3,400.
transform_note: Recorded as a lower-bound injury count only; not converted into deaths.
confidence_note: Useful injury floor, but not a complete casualty accounting.
